p: VFX Toolbox is for visual effects editors and post-production supervisors working with Final Cut Pro.

It provides seven integrated tools for pulling VFX plates from timelines, naming shots, generating title overlays, importing VFX deliveries, extracting shot lists, comparing versions, and exporting metadata codebooks - all driven by FCPXML.
:icon-desktop-do<# alt text #>wnload: Click here to buy on the Mac App Store
VFX Toolbox is a one-time payment of USD$19.99 on the Mac App Store.
VFX Toolbox is currently in public beta whilst we wait for Apple's Approval. You can download it on Apple's TestFlight.
There is no free trial.
Click here to buy on the Mac App Store
You can find information about Educational Pricing here.
CommandPost is The Swiss Army Knife for Post Production Professionals.
It's an open source macOS application that makes your post production and editing life faster and lots more fun (everything is better with more buttons!). 🎛️
It's been used for projects at Netflix, Pixar and extensively at the BBC. People at companies such as Apple, Avid and Adobe use it daily.
All of the 2024 and 2025 Apple Worldwide Developer Conference (WWDC) videos were graded using Tangent panels in Final Cut Pro controlled by CommandPost. 🥳

After 9 years of free updates, to ensure that CommandPost continues to be developed, improved, and stay open-source, we've decided that you need at least ONE paid LateNite application installed to use CommandPost moving forward - consider it "inner circle" software (i.e. only our mates get access to it).
You can use VFX Toolbox to "unlock" CommandPost!
Educational institutions can get a 50% discount when they purchase 20 or more copies through Apple School Manager.
Please contact Apple if you need help with Educational Pricing.
macOS v14.0 (Sonoma) or later
Final Cut Pro v10.8 or later (for FCPXML v1.13 import/export)
Supports .fcpxml files and .fcpxmld bundles
Accepts drag-and-drop from Finder or directly from the Final Cut Pro timeline
Export a complete inventory of every clip in an Final Cut Pro event or project timeline as a tab-delimited file suitable for database import (e.g., FileMaker).
Reads projects, sync-clips, multicam clips, and standalone event clips
Outputs columns for Name, Tape, Camroll, Source TC In/Out, Duration, Record TC In/Out, FPS, Description, and Comments
Automatically discovers and includes all custom metadata fields (Shot Notes, custom columns)
Optional prefix stripping for column headers (e.g., remove MBF_ from metadata field names)
Optional CSV export alongside the primary TXT
Live formatted preview with horizontal scrolling
Change-aware version tracking: appends _v001, _v002, etc., incrementing only when timecode or source reel actually changes
Compound clip wrapping (optional)
Configurable frame handles (head and tail)
Configurable Pull ID and Start Frame fields
Effect preservation -- carries over video/audio filters, color corrections, and spatial transforms
Export
Extract VFX clips from your timeline with handles, versioning, and role assignment. Produces an FCPXML that can be imported directly into Final Cut Pro.
Extraction
Live clip name preview showing the assembled name format
Marker type filters: All, Standard, Chapter, To-Do, or Completed
Naming and Versioning
Open in Final Cut Pro after save
Per-project version history stored in Application Support, with controls to create, reveal, and delete version projects
Plate-type suffix from subrole auto-detection (e.g., _MP01, _BG, _CP) with a dropdown populated from the source FCPXML
Recurses into compound clips and multicam clips to find nested VFX markers
Role assignment with presets (VFX-Pull with date suffix, Custom, or None)
Sidecar CSV with version history for the current export
Three identification modes: Marker Only, Role Only, or Marker & Role
VFX Scan TXT sidecar with Scan Name, VFX ID, Suffix, Start Frame, End Frame, Frame Duration, Handles, Tape, TC range, Pull Date, and Pull ID
Batch-rename VFX clips with sequential shot IDs based on scene metadata, a showcode, and incremental counters. Injects chapter markers carrying the generated shot ID into the FCPXML.
Configurable counter digit padding (2, 3, or 4 digits) and increment step
Configurable scene digit padding (1, 2, or 3 digits)
Configurable target role (default: VFX)
Fallback scene number for clips without scene metadata
Identification
Identify clips by Role, Marker, or Role or Marker
Live example preview (e.g., MBF_093_0010)
Optional vendor code (e.g., RSP)
Options
Overwrite existing IDs or skip clips that already carry a matching shot ID
Reuse missing IDs (fill gaps in numbering) or continue after the highest existing counter
Scene numbers are sanitized (e.g., 104.3 and 104pt2 both resolve to 104)
Shot ID Format
Showcode prefix (e.g., MBF)
Trigger marker type: Chapter, Standard, or To-Do

Generate VFX title overlay clips from markers already present on your timeline. The marker name becomes the Shot ID and the marker note becomes the scope of work. The output is an FCPXML containing a gap with title clips anchored at each marker position, ready to be imported and copied onto your timeline.
Title text format: SHOT_ID -- Scope of Work
Yellow Menlo text with drop shadow on a transparent background
Three position presets: Center, Upper-left (1080p), or Upper-left (2.35:1)
Identification mode: Marker Only, Role Only, or Marker & Role
Marker type filter: All, Standard, Chapter, To-Do, or Completed
Extract a VFX shot list from your timeline as a tab-delimited TXT or a File129-format EDL.
VFX Shot TXT
Columns: VFX Shot ID, Rec TC In/Out, Rec Duration, Frame Count, Src TC In/Out, Src Duration, Reel, Scene, Take, Clip Name, Marker Name, Marker Comment, plus any custom metadata
Auto-detects frame rate from the FCPXML format resource
Recurses into compound clips, multicam clips (active video angle preferred), and sync-clips
Optional CSV export alongside TXT
File129 EDL
Standard EDL header with title and FCM line
Marker names serve as clip slates
Source and record timecodes, reel names, LOC comments with marker color
Shared Options
Identification mode: Marker Only, Role Only, or Marker & Role
Marker type filter with role checkboxes auto-populated from the source FCPXML
Match VFX delivery files back to your timeline by marker name and place them as anchored clips in a new FCPXML project.
Drop a source timeline FCPXML and a folder of VFX files (MOV, MP4, MXF, M4V)
Matching by marker name: exact case-insensitive match, then partial match (filename starts with marker name)
All versions of each shot are stacked (sorted by version ascending, latest on top)
Configurable handle frame trimming and slate frame compensation (+1 frame)
Marker type filter: All, Standard, Chapter, To-Do, or Completed
Role assignment with presets: VFX, Comp, VFX Shots, or Custom
Keyword tagging with the delivery folder name
Live preview showing matched files, unmatched markers, and unmatched files
Open in Final Cut Pro after save
Auto-generated Change Notes column summarizing field-level differences
Auto-switches input mode when you drop an FCPXML onto TXT mode or vice versa
Color-coded rows: green (Identical), yellow (Changed), orange (TC Change), red (Omitted), blue (Added)
Compare two versions of a VFX shot list and generate a color-coded XLSX spreadsheet report.
Comparison Logic
Extra columns inserted after VFX Shot ID: Scope of Work, Notes, Change Notes
FCPXML: compare two FCPXML sequences directly (extracts shots on the fly)
Five status categories: Identical, Changed, Rec TC Change (timecode shifted but duration unchanged), Added, Omitted
Input Modes
Live preview updates as soon as both files are dropped
Native XLSX (OOXML ZIP archive) compatible with Excel and Numbers without warnings
Output
Per-cell change highlighting for Changed and TC Change rows
Shots keyed by VFX Shot ID (first column)
Two worksheets: VFX Comparison (full shot detail) and Summary (status counts)
TXT Files: compare two previously exported VFX Shot TXT files
UX
In Final Cut Pro, add markers (Chapter, Standard, or To-Do) to each VFX clip and assign VFX subroles as needed.
Export your timeline as FCPXML, or drag the project directly from Final Cut Pro into VFX Toolbox.
Open the VFX Pull tab and drop the FCPXML onto the source zone.
Configure extraction: choose the identification mode, marker type, and frame handles.
Under Naming & Versioning, enable version tracking and select or create a project. Choose a plate suffix from the subrole dropdown if applicable.
Under Export, set the Pull ID and Start Frame, and enable the Scan TXT and/or Sidecar CSV exports.
Click Save VFX Pull FCPXML and import the result into Final Cut Pro.
In Final Cut Pro, mark your VFX clips with markers or assign a VFX role.
Export the timeline as FCPXML and drop it onto the VFX Naming tab.
Enter your showcode (e.g., MBF), optional vendor code, scene/counter padding, and increment step.
Choose the identification mode and trigger marker type.
Review the preview and click Save Named FCPXML.
Import the named FCPXML back into FCP. Each clip now carries a chapter marker with the generated shot ID.
Ensure your timeline markers carry shot IDs as names and scope-of-work descriptions as notes.
Export the timeline and drop it onto the VFX Titles tab.
Choose the position preset and marker filter.
Click Generate VFX Titles and import the output FCPXML into FCP.
Select all the title clips in the new event and paste them onto your timeline.
Export your current timeline as FCPXML.
Open the VFX Delivery tab. Drop the FCPXML onto the Source Timeline zone and the delivery folder onto the VFX Folder zone.
Review the match preview. Set handle frames, slate frame trimming, and the role.
Click Save VFX Delivery and import the result into Final Cut Pro. All matched files appear as connected clips at their marker positions.
Drop your FCPXML onto the VFX Shot List tab.
Choose the output format (VFX Shot TXT or File129 EDL), identification mode, and marker filter.
Click Export. Optionally enable "Also export as CSV" for the TXT format.
Open the VFX Compare tab and select the input mode (TXT Files or FCPXML).
Drop the previous version on the left and the current version on the right.
For FCPXML mode, configure the identification mode, marker filter, and roles.
Click Compare & Export XLSX. Open the spreadsheet to review color-coded changes.
Drop an Final Cut Pro event or project FCPXML onto the Codebook tab.
Optionally enter a prefix to strip from metadata column headers.
Click Export Codebook TXT. Enable "Also export as CSV" if needed.

VFX Toolbox has been created by Sam Pluemacher.

You can learn more about LateNite's creations over at FCP Cafe.
This is the privacy policy for the VFX Toolbox, macOS Application that is available on the macOS App Store and TestFlight.
VFX Toolbox does not collect any user data. No personal information is transmitted, stored, or used by this application.
VFX Toolbox does not use any third-party analytics or tracking tools. It does not contain any in-app purchases or advertisements.
Apple may collect and use certain usage and performance data from the apps that are available on the App Store. This data is used to help improve the App Store and the overall user experience.
This data collection is not specific to any individual user, and it is not possible for app developers to opt out of this data collection by Apple.
You can read Apple's App Store Privacy Policy here.
We use this GitHub Repository for documentation hosting and technical support. You can read GitHub's Privacy Policy here.
If you have any questions or concerns, you can submit an issue, or email us: support@latenitefilms.com
